title: Lesson 390
transcript: Continuing with our architecture discussion on networks, we've talked about network topologies, we talked about transport architecture, Let's see how devices can actually communicate with other devices specifically. I think you'll find this easy to understand obviously based on the terminology that the industry uses. So how can a specific device talk to other devices is what we call transmission methods, sometimes referred to as LAN transmission methods. But obviously, if you focus on these words like unicast, that means one device talking specifically to one other device. That's unicast transmissions. One to many is called multicast. And one to all is called multicast. Broadcast, of course, right? Now, again, we have to relate this to a security perspective, right? And on the surface here, if you ask, okay, which of these are more secure? You have to be careful with answering that question because most people would think, okay, well, Unicast is obviously more secure. And you'd be correct if we're thinking about that from a confidentiality perspective, right? So obviously, if you can restrict the transmission to go from one device specifically to another device, that is best from a confidentiality perspective. But what about an availability perspective, which is also a focus of security? then obviously in that case, if the exam, for example, asks you which of these is more secure, that's not enough context to be able to answer the question, right? Because if the exam question is talking about security from a availability perspective, then obviously one to all would be more secure from an availability perspective. So the lesson here is to obviously read the context of the question. Don't automatically assume that if the exam is focusing on security, it's always focusing on confidentiality. Remember, Security really means five things now, right? Confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, and non-repudiation, right? So the exam question has to give you more context before you can answer which of these would be more secure. Alright? And there's actually another one that is specifically mentioned in the exam outline as far as transmission methods are concerned. And that is what we call anycast. Anycast transmission. And if you focus on the word Anycast, this is where incoming requests are forwarded to any number of different locations or a variety of different locations. All right. So we need to be able to decide where we're going to send that because it could go to any device, for example. That's what Anycast means, all right? So incoming requests are forwarded to any number or variety of locations. This is especially useful for architectures like CDNs, content distribution networks, like Netflix, for example, right? Where obviously incoming requests are going to be forwarded to the nearest or best node or device based on obviously providing more efficiency and less latency and in some cases more security as well. All right, so anycast means to any. We have to make that decision. But typically, especially used in CDN networks, we're sending it to the nearest or best within a group. All right. Focused on whatever it is that we may be focusing on, whether it's fastest or more secure and so on. We decide which of the nodes is going to be faster, maybe the nearest and so on. Right. So that's what any cast transmission is all about.
